posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
Kapscandy can be old world for style and not overbearingly fruity.

Spottswoode has great structure and balance. (I am surprised Parker liked it so much.)

You may like the Alpha Omega single vineyard cabs, and the Pott cabs are somewhat similar.

Roy Piper’s cabs are also well put together.

Myriad, Quivet, and carter are great, but more modern Cali in style.

If you like EMH, Rivers Marie is also structured for balance. Robert Young also makes very good reds.

Pahlmeyer, Forman, and Arrowood also bring great construction to the table.

For a glimpse of what great ‘old style’ Cali cab is like, look for A Rafanelli.

Those would all be a fine start.

Harvest “early” compared to the neighbors, work in the vineyard and taste taste taste, and go forward, just trying to not screw it up. Recent vintages come in at 23.3Brix to 24.9. It’s my wheelhouse, what my vineyard wants to do. And guess what? It’s what I like and my customers have come to expect.

Why bother? Amazingly high real estate prices make it harder to find great value in California Cabernet than in almost any region outside of Burgundy. And even if you ignore the high prices, very few California producers focus on “complexity, value, and finesse”, because most California wine consumers care much more about fruit, sugar, and tannins. With the whole world to choose from, why would any thinking Burgundy drinker waste any time at all on California Cabernet when there are so many other more fruitful regions to explore?

Aaron Pott for a Burgundy drinker? Alpha Omega? Myriad/Quiver/Carter/Mike Carter wines? Some of these recommendations make absolutely no sense at all…

posts.name: T 0 m Altmayer
posts.cooked:
Lots of good suggestions, here are some of my thoughts on the wines suggested:

Ridge – The standard bearer for balance and finesse.

Corison – definitely more on the old world side. I recently had a 2010 Napa and it was beautiful although they are better with age.

Montelena – Old world stylings, but still a Napa Cab. IMO, this wine is still as good as Napa gets at that price range.

Spottswoode – older Spottswoode wines tended to be more old world in style but that changed a few years ago. These are not fruit bombs, but they are not as delicate and pretty as they used to be. They are very, very good, just different.

Mayacamas – very much old style, but I’ve had too many tannic and austere monsters that never seemed to come out of their shell.

Forman – a somewhat overlooked producer that really focuses on balance. I enjoy these wines quite a bit.

Mondavi Reserve – older Mondavi Reserves are some of the finest wines I’ve tasted out of Napa. Haven’t bought anything since the middle 2000s, but older ones can be found easily at auction and are great.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
As a fellow Burgundy lover, here is what I know. I love Ridge. The Monte Bello is great, but so are all their Cabs and most of their Zins (esp. the Geyserville and Lytton Springs). I also love Montelena, although it is a more powerful wine. Think of Montelena more as LLC and Ridge more as Ducru, IMHO.

I have not had much Forman, but loved the old Sterling Cabs (1970-1974) from Ric Forman. So, worth a shot.

I have had some very good wines from Mayacamas, but probably have not liked them as much as Monte Bello and Montelena. Historically, very, very traditional and really old vines, but there has been a recent change in ownership so not sure whether anything has changed.

A very old California producer that is legendary for Chardonnay, Stony Hill, started making Cabernet a few years ago. I have only tasted one Cabernet - a 2007. It was really promising, although of course far from ready to drink. I have been buying these in more recent vintages.

Note that some of these are like top Bordeaux in that they need a lot of time to mature and get the types of characteristics you are looking for. My experience with Montelena and Dominus, for example, is that they really need 20+ years to mature. A 1991 Montelena was drinking really well earlier this year and so was a 1978.

I have not had a Mount Eden Cabernet but this one interests me.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Daniel H:

Why bother? Amazingly high real estate prices make it harder to find great value in California Cabernet than in almost any region outside of Burgundy. And even if you ignore the high prices, very few California producers focus on “complexity, value, and finesse”, because most California wine consumers care much more about fruit, sugar, and tannins. With the whole world to choose from, why would any thinking Burgundy drinker waste any time at all on California Cabernet when there are so many other more fruitful regions to explore?

Aaron Pott for a Burgundy drinker? Alpha Omega? Myriad/Quiver/Carter/Mike Carter wines? Some of these recommendations make absolutely no sense at all…

The same thing can be said about Bordeaux - and be equally wrong. You sound like someone with a shallow knowledge based on what the critics like. But, this thread is asking for “great” Cabs that aren’t critic pandering. I agree some of the suggestions just look like favorites rather than what the OP is asking for. But there are plenty of traditionally ripe Cabs being made, some of which are consistently “great” imo. They tend to not be able to command those higher prices and top scores that the super-ripe and dense oaky messes do, but that’s not exactly a problem for consumers who like them and buy to drink.

There are some excellent recommendations so far (+1 Corison, Mount Eden), but there is a new kid on the block (so to speak) that is putting out an absolutely stunning bottle of Cabernet Sauvignon from the Farella Vineyard in Coombsville. If you can get your hands on the 2013 Di Costanzo Cabernet Sauvignon Farella Vineyard, this is truly a gem. The Farella Vineyard used to be a source for Opus One and Mondavi Reserve, and now Massimo di Costanzo (formerly at Screaming Eagle) is crafting one of the best, most balanced, under-the-radar 2013s in all of Napa Valley. At $85 per bottle, this is one of the best QPRs of the vintage imo.

posts.name: Ross W
posts.cooked:
I can agree with alot of the recommendations on this thread as I love the old world cali cabs with finesse.I prefer most of these with some age on them (like a Grand Cru Burgundy or Bordeaux)

Ridge - As others have mentioned, is the pinnacle of complex elegant wines from California. If I could only drink one wine for the rest of my life, it would be Monte Bello. Their other cabs are also fantastic and are not far behind in terms of quality.

Montelena Estate Cab. I agree with the comment earlier that it’s like a 2nd growth bordeaux made in California. Very complex with a distinct eucalyptus nose. A lot to like here once in the bottle for 20 years.

Older Mayacamas - Not much to add to what others have said. I love these wines and wish they still were made the same way (I know…global warming, Andy Erickson, living in the past etc…)

Togni - a little bigger than the three mentioned above, but plenty for a burgundy lover to enjoy as its unique and earthy. Some vintages have a little too much bell pepper…but I dig that.

Heitz Martha’s Vineyard - Expensive, but worth every penny in certain vintages. I have not had a recent one, but the olders ones are mind blowing.

Dunn Howell Mountain. Needs a ton of time, but in the same class as the others and also need a ton of time in the bottle.

Corison - I find them a little “brighter” than the others I mentioned, but always very good.

Older Laurel Glen Cab. Great value but I think the more recent vintages are modern and not as good as the older stuff. You can find older bottles for around $50 ish

Anderson’s Conn Valley Eloge (blend) and reserve cab. I love these wines as I find them drinkable, smooth/silky and complex. Maybe not as “old School” as the others, but one of my go to wines and several Burg drinking friends love them as well.

Stoney Hill is another one that was mentioned and they also happen to be one of my favorite wineries, but mainly for their whites. The cab is very good and hope it develops into something special with time.

posts.name: P@ul Int@g
posts.cooked:
Fellow Berserker, Thomas Bougetz might have something that fits the bill. I tried the 2012 Eminence that was offered at $39 on Berserker Day and found it to be an absolutely fantastic non-spoofed Napa Cab. My note:

  • 2012 Bougetz Cellars Cabernet Sauvignon Eminence - USA, California, Napa Valley (2/15/2016)
    Really loved this - I don’t drink much Napa Cab but found this to be right in my wheelhouse. Very nice nose, though a little shy, showing some intriguing herbal notes, eucalyptus, and red currant. On the palate beautifully balanced with pleasing perfectly ripe red fruit flavors and interesting Cab herbal flavors. Just a hint of oak that integrated rapidly with air. Very precise, medium-bodied for a Napa Cab. Light, lithe mouth feel but plenty of concentration. Fine, sweet tannins. Pleasing finish. Tasted over 2 days (vacuvin’d overnight) - probably slightly better on the 2nd day but, overall, ready to go from the pop 'n pour. Fans of bigger, oakier, Napa Cabs probably would not like this as much as I did, but I prefer lighter, less oaked wines. A Burgundy Lover’s Napa Cab. I’d love to see how this develops over the next 5 - 10 years. (95 pts.)

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Well.

Experiment number one has concluded with a success.

Smith-Madrone

Spring Mountain Fruit

Vintage 2012 - From what some consider a weaker vintage. Not as much complexity as I would like but texturally this is fabulous. Great balance and finesse to me. No coffee that slams you in the face. With coaxing you get that really fantastic fruit profile on the nose. Hard to describe it. I get it in Togni and Spring Mountain wines. Like a really delicious fruit wrapped earthy component. I would assume it is part of the terroir. This wine is wonderful for the tariff. Big winner. Thanks!

Vintage 2013- Very assertive. Again with time and aeration the fruit is that earthy wrapped redder fruit. Like Togni in many ways. Beautiful balance. More complex than the 2012 by a factor of several degrees. To me the extraction is perfect. Sexy texture. Great finishing freshness. The alcohol is higher for sure but still not cloying. (I do like Chateauneufs also) A complete wine from Spring Mountain. Glorious for the tariff. Thank you guys so much. This is exactly what I was asking. This is a pretty big year but this wine is just perfectly put together to my palate. If you like them big and broad however you might not consider this your cup of tea. For this Burg drinker, this is right in my wheelhouse. This will age it seems.

BTW, I consider the 1991 Dominus one of the greatest California wines I have ever tasted.
It was picked as the 1990 Latour beside the 1990 Latour and a few others in a blind tasting. It is that good.

Fellow Berserker, Thomas Bougetz might have something that fits the bill. I tried the 2012 Eminence that was offered at $39 on Berserker Day and found it to be an absolutely fantastic non-spoofed Napa Cab. My note:

  • 2012 Bougetz Cellars Cabernet Sauvignon Eminence - USA, California, Napa Valley (2/15/2016)
    Really loved this - I don’t drink much Napa Cab but found this to be right in my wheelhouse. Very nice nose, though a little shy, showing some intriguing herbal notes, eucalyptus, and red currant. On the palate beautifully balanced with pleasing perfectly ripe red fruit flavors and interesting Cab herbal flavors. Just a hint of oak that integrated rapidly with air. Very precise, medium-bodied for a Napa Cab. Light, lithe mouth feel but plenty of concentration. Fine, sweet tannins. Pleasing finish. Tasted over 2 days (vacuvin’d overnight) - probably slightly better on the 2nd day but, overall, ready to go from the pop 'n pour. Fans of bigger, oakier, Napa Cabs probably would not like this as much as I did, but I prefer lighter, less oaked wines. A Burgundy Lover’s Napa Cab. I’d love to see how this develops over the next 5 - 10 years. (95 pts.)

Don, I would agree with Ridge, Seavey and Corison and as you already know, Dominus. 85 Dominus is one of the best CA Cabs I’ve had. It took a long time to come around but was stunning once it did.

I’m going out on a limb to say that if you like Rioja, you might try an older (20+ year) Silver Oak. There’s a myth that they are fruit bombs but in reality the fruit is well balanced. They are oaky but if you like that style of old Rioja (and to some extent Ridge), they can be really nice. I’ve seen 85 SO beat some pretty impressive competition in blind tastings. And generally the prices are fair because many people feel they have to be drunk with a bag over your head.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
The three that forst jumped to mind for me are:

Drinkward Peschon
Philip Togni
Cathy Corison

Dominus has changed in style since about 2008 forward and I don’t think most recent vintages are in the same psuedo bordeaux style of bygone years.

Corison sells from her library. You should be able to find bottles from a few vintages back that are pretty rocking right now. Such wonderfully balanced wines with a flavor profile often more savory than sweet.

Togni is wonderful with a little age on it.

Mayacamas is another tossed around. I’ve had some back vintage Mayacamas from the late 90s/early 2000s over the past couple of years and have not been that impressed. I will not personally buy anymore of it until someone else’s stash catches me off guard. It’s “old school” in that it’s higher acidity and lower fruit concentration…but the wines I tried lacked some of the balance I’d expect in a $200 bottle of wine perfectly stored for 15-19 years.

posts.name: Jean Hoefliger
posts.cooked:
Well guys,

Sorry for my silence but since it has been a very compact harvest, I had to make wine. I love seeing debates about all this and even for the ones are not fans of the wines. Let me correct a few things with some facts. Yes we do indeed use barrel fermentation up right, I was the first to do that starting already in 2001 as a trial at Newton. It works quite well to integrate tannins and oak. I started with about 3 barrels in 2001 and in 2016 I did 727 barrels for different clients.
Some discussion were about tannins and Napa sexy aspect, just so you know I had to work with the lab equipment company because they could not read the wines that I make because of the high number of tannins and anthocyanin. They said that we had more than ever seen in their data base. Which again does not say that the wines I make are better but for sure different.
If you want to come and discuss the fact that after 3 days in cold soak we are sometimes at levels up to 2100mg/l of tannins, that is for sure not a version of Blockbuster to me.
As usual I really love talking about it so if you are in the valley reach out and lets taste together

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
My second experiment.

2002 Cathy Corison Cabernet Sauvignon

Dark fruit here. Quite primary on the nose. Not much secondary on the nose on opening. Velvety texture to this wine. The palate is so gloriously balanced. Primary dark fruit even now. There is some mid palate complexity. Mild spice and dust. That is the only way I know how to describe it. The finish is a caress. What tannins are there do not grab you. Not a hair out of place. Still a baby on many fronts. I like this.

2010 Cathy Corison Cabernet Sauvignon

More assertive black fruit on the nose and palate. Some dusty element that adds complexity and interest. Less complex than the 2002 but a baby just like that one. This one has more assertiveness and a long finish. Velvet texture with a finish that caresses. More finishing tannin than the 2002. A really nice wine in the making. I like this very much too!

Two more winners.

Thanks to all of you.

I really like these wines.

I am still stuck on that fruit from the Smith-Madrone however.
I love that stuff. Spring Mountain fruit doesn’t get too much love sometimes.
It is so good!

Next up.

???

If you enjoyed the Spring Mountain fruit, try some cabs from Pride from their 1995 and 1996 vintages. Pride went along with Parker and seemed to get bigger and bigger there in the late 90s and early 2000s with Robert Foley at the helm (many incredibly good but rather big wines there), before adjusting their style in more recent years (I want to say around 2008 forward) and making more balanced wines. Their library cab franc is, to quote Trump, “just tremendous.” Some of the best in the valley in my opinion.

I would also suggest late 90s (well, 1994 -1999) and early 2000s (01-02) Dominus for something with a little more progress.

On the Corison, if you like the 2002 bought found it a little primary, try the 2001. It should have a slightly more aged, less primary, and more savory component. It’s just one vintage, but the wine should show a bit differently than the very rich 02 vintage.

I would also strongly, strongly suggest hunting down some great condition bottles of 1997 Chateau Montelena Estate Cab. It’s really a lovely wine with a beautiful bright cherry profile that is developing some wonderful notes of age. A great example of nice Napa cabernet sauvignon without going into the very-dense blue-black fruit profiles that have become quite popular.

posts.name: John Lockwood
posts.cooked:
dcornutt:

What producers make great Cabs for those of us who prize complexity, balance and finesse over power and extraction?
I appreciate any information here.
From a Burgundy drinker.

Don, our 2013 Enfield Fort Ross - Seaview Cab is 13.7% (naturally, no water add), ripe but not overly extracted and only about 15% new oak. It’s a pretty remarkable vineyard, up on a rocky ridge looking west at Marcassin and Hirsch, and its farmed entirely by hand by the couple that owns it - Jesus and Patricia - organic, dry farmed, no till. It was respectfully made to reflect the style of some of the great California Cabs of the 60’s, 70’s and 80’s, but it has its own personality and softness that comes from the coast.

Cheers -
